nativeRange.habitat: Found in slow-moving or stagnant lowland waters including ponds, swamps, rice paddies, flooded fields, rivers, and canals. The species tolerates severely low-oxygen conditions through a specialized air-breathing organ that allows it to breathe atmospheric air directly.

decorPreferences: large caves or PVC pipe hides proportionate to adult size
robust or artificial plants (will uproot live plants)
smooth substrate (sand or fine gravel) — scaleless belly is abrasion-sensitive
very secure tight-fitting lid (walks out of open tanks)
subdued lighting with plenty of cover

diet.notes: The Walking Catfish is an opportunistic predator and scavenger that in the wild feeds on fish, frogs, invertebrates, and organic detritus. In captivity it generally accepts large sinking pellets, carnivore sticks, earthworms, cut fish, shrimp, mussel, and other meaty foods. Any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth is at risk of being eaten. Portions should be kept modest, as this species tends to become obese when overfed. Feeder goldfish are best avoided due to their high thiaminase content, which can deplete vitamin B1 over time.

goodWith: similarly sized robust catfish of comparable size (with caution)
large cichlids above 30 cm (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten (virtually all community fish)
plants (will be uprooted and may be eaten)
conspecifics in confined spaces (territorial and cannibalistic)

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species photo; typical wild-type brown colouration with pale lateral spots.
Clear lateral view of adult specimen; good detail of flattened head and barbels.

summary: The Walking Catfish (Clarias batrachus) is a large, air-breathing predatory catfish from Southeast Asia capable of moving overland on its pectoral fins. It is banned for sale or possession in Florida, Hawaii, and several other US states. Scaleless and copper-sensitive, it is recommended for specialist keepers only.
careNotes:
The Walking Catfish is one of the most notorious invasive fish species in the United States, having established wild populations throughout Florida following aquarium releases in the 1960s. It is currently banned for import, sale, or possession in Florida, Hawaii, and several other US states, as well as being restricted in various countries. Prospective keepers must verify local legality before acquisition.

This species possesses a suprabranchial accessory breathing organ that functions analogously to a labyrinth organ, allowing it to breathe atmospheric air and survive in severely hypoxic water or even out of water for extended periods (hours to days in humid conditions). This same adaptation enables it to ‘walk’ across damp land using its strong, spiny pectoral fins to lever itself forward — escaping open-top tanks is a serious and well-documented hazard. A very heavy, tight-fitting lid with no gaps is non-negotiable.

Adult size reaches 30–60 cm (occasionally reported larger in optimal conditions), requiring a tank of at least 500 L with a 180 cm footprint. It is entirely scaleless, making it highly sensitive to copper-based medications — any copper treatments in a shared system can be lethal. Substrate should be soft sand or fine smooth gravel to prevent abrasion of the unprotected belly.

It is an ambush predator and voracious scavenger. Any fish, crustacean, or invertebrate small enough to be swallowed will be eaten. Even similarly-sized conspecifics carry cannibalism risk in confined quarters. This is effectively a species-only specimen fish requiring an advanced keeper with a very large, dedicated setup.
breedingNotes: The Walking Catfish has been bred extensively in aquaculture across Asia for food production, but captive breeding in hobbyist aquaria is extremely rare and not practical in a home setting. In the wild, spawning is triggered by the onset of the monsoon season, with males guarding nests built in flooded vegetation. Commercial aquaculture operations typically induce spawning through hormonal injection using HCG or carp pituitary extract — a technique that is not realistically achievable outside of specialist aquaculture facilities.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Clarias batrachus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 60 cm), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range, diet, air-breathing organ biology, and invasive-status notes for Florida and other regions.

additional.name: FishBase — Clarias batrachus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Clariidae, Siluriformes), adult size (to 60 cm TL), temperature (22–28°C), native range countries, and confirmed obligate air-breather status. Lifespan and invasive range data also referenced.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Walking Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-facing care summary. Confirmed escape risk, copper sensitivity (scaleless), tank size requirements, diet recommendations, and specialist-only designation.

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species — Clarias batrachus
additional.notes: US Geological Survey invasive species database. Documents established Florida populations, history of aquarium-trade introductions beginning 1960s, and state-level legal restrictions across Florida, Hawaii, and other states.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Walking Catfish profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist source. Confirmed care level (advanced/specialist), walking behaviour via pectoral spines, air-breathing organ description, minimum tank footprint guidance, and legal-restriction advisory.

fish.breedingNotes: The Walking Catfish is commercially bred in Asian aquaculture through hormonal induction, but this process is not feasibly reproducible in a home aquarium. In the wild, spawning is triggered by the monsoon season, with males guarding nests in flooded vegetation. Breeding is not realistically achievable in a home setting.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Established invasive in Florida; import/possession banned in many US states (e.g., Florida, Hawaii) and globally. Reaches 60+ cm; obligate air-breather capable of crossing dry land between bodies of water.
